title-group.article-title: Left Ventricular Outflow Tract Geometry as a Predictor of Permanent Pacemaker Implantation After Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation Using the Evolut Series

aff.institution: Galway University Hospitals and University of Galway

aff.named-content: Royal College of Surgeons in Ireland (RCSI)
aff.institution: University of Medicine and Health Sciences

aff.named-content: Precision Cardiovascular Medicine & Innovation Institute (PCMI), Cardiovascular Research Institute (CVRI)
Department of Cardiology, Amsterdam UMC, Amsterdam Cardiovascular Sciences

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Catheterization and Cardiovascular Interventions published by Wiley Periodicals LLC.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Predicting the need for permanent pacemaker implantation (PPM) after transcatheter aortic valve implantation (TAVI) is crucial for procedural planning and patient counseling.

sec.title: Aims
sec.p: To evaluate whether left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT) morphology, specifically a tapered configuration, predicts conduction disturbances requiring PPM after TAVI using the Evolut self‐expanding valve.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This retrospective multicenter study included patients undergoing TAVI with the self‐expanding Evolut series. Pre‐TAVI MSCT analysis included LVOT shape, membranous septum (MSep) length, and landing‐zone calcification. Implantation depth (ID) was measured on final aortography.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: A total of 189 patients were included (age, 79.2 ± 6.8 years, 46% female, EuroSCORE II, 3.1 [1.9, 5.6]). Seventeen patients (9%) required PPM at 30 days. Patients with a tapered LVOT had a threefold higher PPM rate compared with those with a non‐tapered configuration (21% vs. 7%, p = 0.015). PPM was significantly associated with tapered LVOT (OR: 4.2, p = 0.041), pre‐existing right bundle branch block (RBBB) (OR: 12.4, p = 0.001), and deep valve implantation with a short MSep (OR: 16.4, p < 0.001).

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Tapered LVOT configuration, pre‐existing RBBB, and deep implantation in the context of a short MSep are independent predictors of PPM following TAVI with the Evolut TAVI platform. Integrating LVOT configuration and MSep length into preprocedural planning may improve patient selection and prediction of PPM.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Transcatheter aortic valve implantation (TAVI) has become an established therapeutic option for patients with severe aortic stenosis, especially those considered at high or prohibitive surgical risk. More recently, randomized trials have demonstrated favorable outcomes with TAVI even in low‐risk patients, expanding its use across a broader clinical spectrum [1, 2].

Despite advances in valve technology and implantation techniques, conduction disturbances remain the most frequent complication following TAVI. These are associated with increased morbidity, mortality, longer hospital stays, and higher healthcare costs [3]. Among these complications, the need for permanent pacemaker implantation (PPM) is particularly concerning, with reported rates ranging from 8.5% to 25.9%, depending on the prosthesis type. Notably, self‐expanding valves—such as those in the Evolut series (Medtronic, Minneapolis, MN, USA)—have been consistently associated with higher PPM rates compared to balloon‐expandable systems [4, 5].

The underlying pathophysiology of post‐TAVI conduction disturbances is primarily mechanical. The bundle of His, which runs through the membranous septum (MSep) adjacent to the left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT) and just below the aortic annulus, is particularly susceptible to injury during valve deployment. Compression or stretching of this region by the prosthesis—especially with the self‐expanding design's radial force and deeper implantation—has been implicated in the development of high‐grade atrioventricular block and subsequent pacemaker requirement [6].

Several anatomical and procedural factors have been associated with increased PPM risk, including short MSep length, pre‐existing conduction abnormalities, implantation depth (ID), valve oversizing, and LVOT eccentricity. Additionally, a higher prosthesis‐to‐LVOT diameter ratio has been linked to persistent pacing needs, particularly in patients receiving self‐expanding valves [7, 8].

Given the critical anatomical relationship between the LVOT and the conduction system, we hypothesize that LVOT morphology, particularly when expressed relative to the aortic annulus area, may be a significant predictor of conduction disturbances requiring PPM after TAVI with self‐expanding valves. Therefore, this study aims to evaluate the association between preprocedural LVOT geometry and the incidence of PPM at 30 days following implantation of self‐expanding transcatheter valves, focusing on the Evolut series.

sec.title: Study Design and Patient Population
sec.p: This is a multicenter, retrospective, observational study. Consecutive patients who underwent TAVI with the Medtronic Evolut R/PRO/PRO+ system at three centers in Ireland and Egypt were screened for inclusion eligibility. Patients with prior PPM, those undergoing a valve‐in‐valve procedure, and patients who received > 1 valve during the index procedure were excluded. The study complied with the ethical guidelines of the Declaration of Helsinki and was approved by the institutional ethical committee. Clinical outcomes were defined based on the Valve Academic Research Consortium‐3 (VARC‐3) consensus document [9].

sec.title: TAVI Procedure and Periprocedural Care
sec.p: Preprocedure workup included baseline ECG, transthoracic echocardiography (TTE), and multislice computed tomography (MSCT). The heart team determined eligibility for TAVI in all cases. The TAVI procedure was performed in accordance with the instructions for use and the hospital's standard procedure. Valve release was performed under fast or rapid pacing, aiming at a final ID of 3−5 mm. Sedative anesthesia, transfemoral access, and cusp overlap technique constituted the default strategy. Pre‐ and/or post‐dilatation were performed at the discretion of the operator. Standard care post‐TAVI included daily ECG until discharge as well as a pre‐discharge echocardiogram. The indication of PPM was done according to the guidelines and local hospital protocol [10].

sec.title: MSCT Analysis
sec.p: Pre‐TAVI MSCT was analyzed according to the recommendations of the Society of Cardiovascular Computed Tomography [11, 12]. The reconstruction and analysis were performed using the 3mensio Structural Heart software program (Pie Medical Imaging, Maastricht, The Netherlands).

The LVOT was measured at 3 mm from the annular plane. The morphology of the landing zone was classified into two categories according to the ratio between the aortic annulus (AA) and the LVOT sizes. Those with a tapered landing zone had an AA area ≥ 10% greater than the LVOT area, while those with a non‐tapered anatomy had an AA area < 10% greater than the LVOT area. This latter group was subclassified into a tubular morphology (similar areas of both AA and LVOT) or a flared morphology (AA area smaller than LVOT area) [13].

Calcification of the LVOT was visually graded as none = 0, mild = 1, moderate = 2, and severe = 3. The device landing zone calcium volume (DLZ‐CV) was measured semi‐automatically using a scan‐specific individual threshold derived from the mean attenuation of the ascending aorta plus 4 standard deviations. Calcium volume was measured for the aortic valve (basal plane to above the commissures), AA (3 mm above basal plane and 2 mm below basal plane), and the LVOT (from basal plane to 5 mm below). Calcification was also measured separately for each cusp, and values were obtained in mm [3, 14]. Mitral annular calcification (MAC) was graded as 0 = no MAC, 1 = mild MAC affecting ≤ 25% of the annulus, 2 = moderate MAC affecting 25%−50% of the annulus, or 3 = severe MAC affecting ≥ 50% of the annulus [15].

The LVOT eccentricity index was defined as (1−[minimum diameter/maximum diameter]) ×100. The degree of prosthesis oversizing by perimeter was calculated as [(nominal prosthesis perimeter/annulus perimeter–1) ×100%]. The LVOT oversizing index (OI) was calculated as ([nominal prosthesis perimeter–LVOT perimeter]/nominal prosthesis perimeter) ×100) [16].

sec.title: ID and MSep Length Measurement
sec.p: The ID was determined on the final aortogram post‐TAVI and was measured as the depth from the ventricular edge of the THV frame up to the nadir of the non‐coronary cusp (NCC) [17]. This methodology was adopted by both centers, and the overall median (4 mm) was taken to categorize ID as “shallow” or “deep.” MSep length was measured using two measurement methods, depending on the participating center, either the stretched vessel view [18], or the coronal view [19] as illustrated in Supporting Information S1: Figure S1. To standardize across centers, MSep length was categorized as “short” or “long” based on the center‐specific median. A composite variable, “deep implantation depth into a short membranous septum,” was created to reflect the combined mechanical and anatomical influence on conduction disturbances.

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: Categorical variables were presented as numbers and percentages. Continuous variables were reported as mean ± standard deviation (SD) or median and quartiles as appropriate. The Shapiro–Wilk test was used to test the normality of continuous variables. Continuous data were compared using Student's t‐test (normality) or Mann–Whitney U test (non‐normality). Categorical data were compared using the chi‐square test or Fisher's exact test. Independent predictors of new PPM were determined using binary logistic regression. Odds ratios (ORs) with their corresponding 95% confidence intervals (CIs) were used to report the results. The variables included in the univariable analysis were TAVI year, left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) < 50%, pre‐existing right bundle branch block (RBBB), tapered LVOT, MAC ≥ moderate, deep ID in short MSep, transcatheter heart valve (THV) type, THV size, LVOT oversizing index, and oversizing by annulus perimeter. The variables included in the multivariable logistic regression analysis were pre‐existing RBBB, tapered LVOT, and deep ID in short MSep. A p value of less than 0.05 in multivariate analysis was considered statistically significant. All statistical analyses were performed using SPSS Statistics for Windows, version 27.0 (SPSS Inc., Chicago, Ill., USA).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Between April 2019 and September 2023, 189 consecutive patients treated with the Medtronic Evolut platform were included in the analysis. The average age was 79.2 ± 6.8 years, and less than one‐half (46%) were female. The median EuroSCORE II was 3.1 [IQR, 1.9−5.6]. All procedures were transfemoral, and all but two were performed under sedative anesthesia. The main study results are summarized in the Central Illustration 1.

caption.p: LVOT Morphology and Risk of Permanent Pacemaker Implantation after TAVI The upper panel summarizes the baseline demographic and clinical characteristics of the study population. The lower left panel illustrates the definition of LVOT morphology and the distribution of tapered versus non‐tapered LVOT anatomy within the cohort. The lower middle panel compares the rate of PPM at 30 days between patients with tapered and non‐tapered LVOT. The lower right panel presents the results of the multivariable logistic regression analysis identifying independent predictors of PPM at 30 days following TAVI with the Evolut platform. [Color figure can be viewed at wileyonlinelibrary.com]

sec.p: Thirty‐three patients had a tapered LVOT configuration (17.5%) while 156 (82%) had a non‐tapered (tubular or flared) LVOT. The two groups had similar baseline clinical, ECG, and echocardiographic characteristics, with the exception of a higher frequency of reduced LV EF in the non‐tapered group. Demographic, clinical, ECG, and echocardiographic data of the overall study cohort are summarized in Supporting Information S1: Table S1, while the comparison between tapered and non‐tapered LVOT geometry is detailed in Table 1.

caption.p: Baseline demographic, clinical, ECG, and echocardiographic characteristics according to LVOT geometry (tapered vs. non‐tapered).

tr.td: NYHA class III or IV, n (%)

fn.p: Abbreviations: AoV = aortic valve, AV block = atrioventricular block, COPD = chronic obstructive airway disease, CVA = cerebrovascular accident, EuroSCORE II = European System for Cardiac Operative Risk Evaluation II, LBBB = left bundle branch block, LCA = left coronary artery, LVEF = left ventricular ejection fraction, LVOT = left ventricular outflow tract, MAC = mitral annular calcification, MI = myocardial infarction, NYHA = New York Heart Association, PPM = permanent pacemaker, RBBB = right bundle branch block, RCA = right coronary artery.

sec.p: Regarding MSepCT characteristics, the LVOT measurements were significantly different, while annular dimensions were not significantly different between the two groups. Patients with a tapered LVOT had markedly smaller LVOT minimum and maximum diameters, perimeter, and area compared to the non‐tapered group (p < 0.01 for all). LVOT eccentricity index trended higher in the tapered group (0.30 ± 0.08 vs. 0.25 ± 0.06), suggesting a relatively more elliptical geometry (p = 0.054). Landing zone calcification (valvular and LVOT) was not significantly different between those with tapered versus non‐tapered LVOT (Table 2). Using quantitative analysis (calcium volume), the total calcific burden in the device landing zone and LVOT, as well as segmental distribution (NCC, RCC, LCC), were similar between groups. On the other hand, ≥ moderate MAC was significantly more frequent in the tapered LVOT group (55% vs. 32%, p = 0.046). Moreover, patients with a tapered LVOT had a shorter MSep (2.4 [1.4, 3.1] vs. 3 [2.3, 4], p = 0.018). Computed tomographic characteristics of the overall study cohort are summarized in Supporting Information S1: Table S2, while the comparison between tapered and non‐tapered LVOT geometry is detailed in Table 2.

caption.p: Preprocedural computed tomographic measurements comparison between the tapered and non‐tapered LVOT geometry.

fn.p: Abbreviations: AoV = aortic valve, DLZ = device landing zone, LBBB = left bundle branch block, LCA = left coronary artery, LCC = left coronary cusp, LVOT = left ventricular outflow tract, MAC = mitral annular calcification, NCC = noncoronary cusp, RCA = right coronary artery, RCC = right coronary cusp.

sec.p: Evolut R was implanted in 50% of the patients, while 27% received Evolut PRO+ and 24% received Evolut PRO. The size 29 mm was used in 59% of the cases. Balloon pre‐dilation was used in 68% and balloon post‐dilation in 37%. The procedural characteristics were similar between the two groups except for the LVOT oversizing index, which was greater in the tapered LVOT group (19.4 ± 6.3) versus (13.4 ± 6.3), p < 0.001. Procedural details of the overall study cohort are summarized in Supporting Information S1: Table S3, while the comparison between tapered and non‐tapered LVOT geometry is detailed in Table 3.

caption.p: Procedural characteristics comparison between the tapered and non‐tapered LVOT geometry.

tr.td: Deep IM in short MSep, n (%)

fn.p: Abbreviations: ID = implantation depth, LVOT = left ventricular outflow tract, MSep = membranous septum, NCC = noncoronary cusp, THV = transcatheter heart valve.

sec.p: Procedural and in‐hospital death occurred in 0% and 2% of patients. Apart from the 30‐day PPM rate, periprocedural complications were similar between the two groups. Periprocedural complications of the overall study cohort are summarized in Supporting Information S1: Table S4, while the comparison between tapered and non‐tapered LVOT geometry is detailed in Table 4.

caption.p: Outcome and in‐hospital complications according to LVOT geometry (tapered vs. non‐tapered).

fn.p: Abbreviations: LBBB = left bundle branch block, LVOT = left ventricular outflow tract, MI = myocardial infarction, PVL = para‐valvular leakage.

sec.p: The overall rate of new PPM was 9% (17/186) at 30 days. All permanent pacemakers were implanted for complete atrioventricular block, except for one patient who developed a new left bundle branch block (QRS duration 179 ms) associated with first‐degree AV block (PR 330 ms). The PPM rate was significantly higher in the tapered LVOT group (21%) compared with the non‐tapered group (7%, p = 0.015). In univariable logistic regression, pre‐existing RBBB, tapered LVOT, deep ID in a short MSep, and moderate‐to‐severe MAC were significantly associated with post‐TAVI PPM (Supporting Information S1: Table S5). To minimize model overfitting given the limited number of events, the final multivariable regression analysis included pre‐existing RBBB, tapered LVOT, and deep ID in a short MSep. On multivariable analysis (Table 5), three independent predictors of PPM emerged: tapered LVOT geometry (OR, 4.2, 95% CI: [1.1–16.7], p = 0.041), pre‐existing RBBB (OR, 12.4, 95% CI: [2.6–58.8], p = 0.001), and deep implantation in patients with a short MSep (OR, 16.4, 95% CI: [3.5–77.2], p < 0.001).

caption.p: Multivariable logistic regression analysis identifying predictors of conduction disturbances requiring PPM at 30 days.

fn.p: Abbreviations: CI = confidence interval, ID = implantation depth, LVOT = left ventricular outflow tract, MSep = membranous septum, PPM = permanent pacemaker implantation, RBBB = right bundle branch block.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The present study investigated the effect of LVOT anatomical and morphological characteristics on the need for PPM after TAVI with the self‐expanding Evolut series. The rate of new PPM was 9% at 30 days. Patients with a tapered LVOT exhibited a threefold higher rate of PPM compared to those with a non‐tapered configuration. Tapered LVOT geometry, pre‐existing RBBB, and deep implantation in the setting of a short MSep emerged as the most important predictors of postprocedural PPM at 30 days.

Among the variables studied, pre‐existing RBBB emerged as a strong and independent predictor of PPM (OR: 12.4, p = 0.001). This aligns with previous studies [7, 8] demonstrating the vulnerability of the conduction system in patients with baseline conduction disturbances, particularly when further mechanical compression occurs during valve deployment. Since the left bundle lies in close proximity to the interventricular septum near the LVOT, patients with existing RBBB have limited conduction reserve and are more susceptible to complete atrioventricular block following TAVI [6].

Another key finding was the association between a tapered LVOT geometry and increased PPM risk (OR: 4.2, p = 0.041). A tapered LVOT likely contributes to asymmetric device expansion and increased radial force toward the MSep, potentiating injury to the nearby conduction tissues [13, 16]. In our cohort, the univariable analysis yielded an odds ratio of 3.9 (95% CI: 1.3–11.0), which remained significant after multivariable adjustment (OR 4.2, 95% CI: 1.1–16.7). The minimal change in effect size after adjustment supports the association between LVOT morphology and post‐TAVI conduction disturbances.

Moreover, we identified that deep valve implantation in patients with a short MS significantly increased the risk of PPM (OR: 16.5, p < 0.001). This finding reinforces the concept that the depth of implantation relative to the MSep length is critical [12, 20]. A short MSep provides minimal anatomical buffer between the prosthesis and the conduction system, especially when the valve is implanted deep, leading to direct trauma or sustained pressure on the His bundle [6].

Several studies have previously explored the relationship between LVOT‐to‐annulus ratio and the risk of conduction disturbances following TAVI [13, 16, 21, 22, 23]. However, meaningful comparisons are limited by significant methodological variability across studies. One key difference lies in the measurement site: while some groups measured LVOT geometry at 5 [13, 16] or 4 mm [23] below the annular plane, others—like the present study—measured LVOT at 3 mm below the annular plane [22]. In addition, the definition of a “tapered” LVOT is inconsistent in the literature. Some studies characterized tapering based on LVOT area [13, 16, 23], while others used diameter measurements [21, 22], which may be more susceptible to geometric distortion and eccentricity. Even among studies using area ratios, the cutoff values vary, ranging from 0.858 to 0.985, depending on ROC‐derived thresholds in some studies [13, 23]. Finally, the type of implanted valve further complicates interpretation. One of the prior studies was based on BEV [13] or a mixed population of BEVs and SEV [16, 23]. The Evolut platform was implanted in 44% of the patients in the Kikuchi et al. study [23], while 1% only in Espejo‐Paeres et al. [16]. In contrast, our study focused exclusively on patients receiving Evolut valves, allowing for a more targeted assessment of anatomical predictors in this specific device category. These devices differ substantially in frame design, radial force distribution, and expansion behavior, all of which influence interaction with the LVOT and surrounding conduction tissues. In Ishizu et al. [13] study, tapered LVOT was identified as an independent predictor for PPM after implantation of the SAPIEN platform, but it was associated with PPM in patients receiving the Evolut platform only in the study by Kikuchi et al. [23].

Our findings have significant clinical implications, particularly when the annulus dimensions fall in the borderline zone between two prosthesis sizes. In such cases, the dimensions of LVOT may play a crucial role in the decision‐making process. For patients with a tapered LVOT who appear to be at higher risk of developing atrioventricular block after TAVI, procedural strategies such as careful valve sizing, consideration of alternative transcatheter platforms, or use of intermediate valve sizes may help reduce conduction disturbance risk. However, these considerations should be validated in larger prospective studies [16, 24].

Overall, our findings support a more nuanced anatomical approach to risk stratification before TAVI, particularly with self‐expanding valves such as the Evolut series. Preprocedural CT assessment of LVOT geometry and MSep length, coupled with real‐time ID control, may help in tailoring procedural strategies to reduce the incidence of PPM [25].

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: The present study is a retrospective observational study of a relatively small sample size, and this may introduce inherent selection and information biases, and limit the statistical power. The limited number of PPM events restricted the extent of our multivariable modeling, resulting in wide CIs for some estimates. As a result, we intentionally narrowed our multivariable model to three predictors. These findings should be viewed as exploratory and require validation in larger prospective cohorts. The primary outcome was the need for PPM, which may not fully capture the spectrum of subclinical or transient conduction abnormalities. The study focused exclusively on self‐expanding valves (Evolut series), which may limit the generalizability of our findings to other TAVI platforms. MSep length was measured using different CT methodologies across centers, which precluded pooling of the raw values. To minimize measurement bias, data were standardized by categorization based on center‐specific medians. Nonetheless, this approach may have reduced precision compared with using uniform raw measurements, and future studies should aim to apply a single standardized imaging method for MSep assessment. ID in this study was measured from final aortography, which may be influenced by the angiographic projection, parallax, and contrast opacification. In addition, annular plane identification can be challenging in the presence of localized calcification or narrow sinuses. Post‐TAVI multidetector CT would provide a more precise and reproducible assessment of ID. Unfortunately, post‐TAVI CT was not systematically performed in our study.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Our study demonstrates that certain anatomical, clinical, and procedural factors—namely, a tapered LVOT geometry, pre‐existing RBBB, and a deep implantation in the setting of a short MSep—are independent predictors of PPM following TAVI with the Evolut platform. These findings underscore the importance of detailed preprocedural imaging and individualized implantation strategies to minimize the risk of conduction disturbances. Incorporating LVOT morphology and MSep length into routine pre‐TAVI assessment may aid in identifying high‐risk patients and optimizing procedural outcomes.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: The authors received no specific funding for this work.

sec.title: Ethics Statement
sec.p: The study was conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki and was approved by the ethics committee of each participating center.

sec.title: Consent
sec.p: Patient consent was waived due to the retrospective nature of the study.

sec.title: Conflicts of Interest
sec.p: Dr. Angela McInerney reports honoraria from Shockwave IVL Inc., Novartis, and Boston Scientific outside the submitted work. Dr. Darren Mylotte is a consultant for Medtronic, Boston Scientific, and MicroPort. Dr. Osama Soliman reports institutional research grants outside the submitted work. The other authors declare no conflicts of interest.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: All data are contained within the article.
